senses.definition: Relating to or derived from carbanil, a chemical compound (not commonly used outside chemistry).
examples: The residue was analyzed for carbanilic impurities.
Carbanilic derivatives were synthesized in the lab.

senses.definition: A chemical compound also known as symmetrical diphenylurea, produced from aniline and urea.
examples: Carbanilid is used in certain chemical syntheses.
The laboratory purchased carbanilid for the reaction.

senses.definition: A silky crystalline compound (C6H5NH)2CO obtained by heating aniline with urea; also called symmetrical diphenyl-urea.
examples: Carbanilide is studied as a model compound in organic chemistry.
The supplier lists carbanilide among its chemical reagents.

senses.definition: A negatively charged carbon atom that acts as a reactive intermediate in organic reactions.
examples: A carbanion can act as a base or nucleophile in reactions.
Stabilized carbanions are useful in carbon–carbon bond formation.

senses.definition: Plural of carbanion; multiple negatively charged carbon species involved in different reactions.
examples: Different carbanions can be generated for various synthetic routes.
These carbanions participate as nucleophiles.

senses.definition: A barn or structure used for storing cars or related equipment; extremely rare or historical usage; not common in modern English.
examples: A carbarn appears in the old town as a shed for carriages.
The term carbarn is mostly found in historical texts.

senses.definition: Plural form of carbarn; historically used to refer to barns for carriages or vehicles, now very rare.
examples: The manuscript mentions several carbarns along the river.
Researchers note the term carbarns in 19th-century inventories.

senses.definition: An insecticide sold under the brand Sevin, used on many crops and in residential settings.
examples: Carbaryl is applied to control beetles and other pests.
Care must be taken when applying carbaryl near water sources.

senses.definition: Plural form of carbaryl; insecticides used on crops and in pest control.
examples: Farmers use carbaryls to protect fruit orchards.
Carbaryls must be handled with protective equipment.

senses.definition: A rare or obscure chemical term; likely a historical or specialized name, not widely used in contemporary general English.
examples: The historian cited the term carbasus in a 19th-century text.

senses.definition: Relating to carbazole or a class of nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds.
examples: Carbazic derivatives were studied for electronic properties.
The carbazic framework is common in certain dyes.

senses.definition: A chemical compound containing a carbazole-derived amide group; used in specialty chemistry.
examples: The reaction yielded a carbazide intermediate.

senses.definition: A chemical substance; may appear in specialized literature, not common in everyday English.
examples: The study mentions carbazin as a reagent.

senses.definition: A chemical compound used in chemistry contexts; sometimes encountered in pharmacology or medicinal chemistry literature.
examples: Carbazine is closely related to carbazine in nomenclature.

senses.definition: A nitrogen-containing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon used in dyes and organic electronics.
examples: Carbazole is a key component in some OLED materials.
Researchers studied the photophysical properties of carbazole derivatives.

senses.definition: Plural form of carbazole; compounds used in dyes and electronics.
examples: The lab synthesized several carbazole derivatives.

senses.definition: Relating to or containing carbazole-like structures; a rare term in chemistry.
examples: The compound displayed carbazylic characteristics in spectroscopy.

senses.definition: A colloquial or historical spelling of 'caribou' or simply a misspelling; not standard modern usage.
examples: Old texts sometimes use carbeen when referring to deer in folklore.

senses.definition: Plural form of carbeen; historical/rare usage; not common in modern English.
examples: The glossary lists several carbeens.
Carbeens appear in older dialect dictionaries.

senses.definition: A highly reactive species in organic chemistry with a divalent carbon atom; often short-lived.
examples: Carbenes are reactive intermediates in many reactions.
The experiment generated a carbene under controlled conditions.

senses.definition: Plural of carbene; reactive carbon species used in organometallic chemistry.
examples: Various carbenes can insert into bonds.
Cyclic carbenes are studied for catalyst design.

senses.definition: A hypothetical or rare compound name; not widely used in standard chemistry references.
examples: The paper mentions a carberry analog named carberry.

senses.definition: A chemical prefix meaning ethyl carbonate or ethoxycarbonyl group; appears in systematic names (e.g., carbethoxy-).
examples: Carbethoxyacetate is the ester formed with ethyl carbonate.
The molecule bears a carbethoxy group.

senses.definition: A chemical group derived from carbethoxy; used in naming some compounds.
examples: The carbethoxyl group is present in several ester derivatives.

senses.definition: A compound of carbon and a metal or semi-metal; used in industry (e.g., calcium carbide).
examples: Calcium carbide is a common carbide used to produce acetylene.
Carbide tools are very hard for cutting.
